sentences.text: Can't wait to leave.
I was going to change my flight.
Can't change your flight.
I want to go home.
I want to get ready for Israel.
Oh, you want me to speak a longer sentence?
Can't wait to go to Israel.
I'd love to see my brother.
Haven't seen him for quite a few years.
And I'm looking forward.
I can't-- And I'm going right after Passover.
But that's not a long--
My name is Rose Leibowitz and I was born in Meserich.
It's in Polish, it's Miedzyrzec.
The town was a Jewish town.
There was about 18,000 Jewish people living there before the war.
It was a-- to my estimation, to my knowledge, it was a town where the Jews-- it was a real Jewish town with Jewish people working.
There-- there were tanneries.
There were bristle factories.
There were all different kind.
And it was a very rich cultural Jewish town with a lot of Jewish schools, a Jewish public school, [INAUDIBLE], Jewish high school.
And we had-- we even had a Jewish hospital in our town.
We had a Jewish fire department.
And it really a very nice town to live in.
My father-- it was my grandfather's place.
And all the family-- part of the family worked in the tannery, part of the family worked in the bristles-- with bristles.
That's what my family was.
They were very nice people, I think.
Everybody had a lot of respect for my family.
And they were nice Jewish people, helpful people to the people in need.
Well, my-- my father, I can't talk too much about my father.
And I-- and I can't talk too much about my mother.
But of course, I lost them when I was very young.
And me and my brother, my brother, which resides now in Israel, we-- we were brought up since we were young kids by an aunt and an uncle which were just like parents to us.
And that was my life before the war.
I Went to school, to public school.
And-- and then my mother passed away.
And I had one year of high school when the war broke out.
And that's-- had a lot of nice, Jewish friends.
We had a lot of Jewish thing going on in the city.
But I was too young to participate in most of the things.
Oh, yes.
My family belonged-- always belong to synagogue.
Always belonged to synagogue.
We were very Jewish-minded.
No.
When I was growing up before the war, no.
Just myself and my-- and my parents and my brother.
And next to us, I had my aunt living.
And across the street, my other aunt, and my grandfather.
We were-- a lot of my family, cousins, they lived on the same street.
Well, that was when the war broke out, we took in a family from Nasielsk.
Their name was Kolinko.
I don't recall the man's name, but the wife's name was [INAUDIBLE].
And they had three sons and a daughter.
The girl's name was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Rachel.
It escapes me now, her first name.
When I get-- she had two names.
[INAUDIBLE] Her mother's name was [INAUDIBLE], and she was Rachel.
Chaya ?] Rachel.
And then she had a brother, which one I recall was Lazar.
The other two, I don't recall their names.
And they stayed with us until they took them away to the concentration camps or whatever.
I used to run in and look at it, but I never had an opportunity to work.
I was too young.
No, we couldn't work there.
My brother was involved with Betar organizations.
Well, he was a very young fella which had his mind set on being-- being a Jabotinsky man.
And that's what he is until today.
No.
I had-- I had quite a few gentile girl friends.
I think our town was, it was nice.
The people were nice.
There wasn't that many gentiles.
I'm ashamed I don't have the picture from my school class which I have back in Illinois.
But there was-- we were a nice group of children.
Not in Meserich.
Not in Meserich.
There was no-- to my knowledge, there was no anti-Semitism in Meserich.
Maybe I was too young to understand it, but the thing is the people that I came in contact with, they were all nice.
They were all nice.
Oh, people were always talking about what's going on in Germany.
In 19-- just before Passover of 1939, they thought there was going to be a war.
[DOORBELL] And then it changed.
[BACKGROUND CHATTER] Then it changed.
He's going to be worried about me, whether to answer the door.
[CLANKING] Before the war, I was only a young girl, and I didn't do nothing special.
I just went to school, stayed with my friends, visited.
They come to visit us.
I was with my family.
We were a very close-- close family.
Very close.
My aunts, my mother's sisters, and my mother had an only brother.
He was a wonderful, wonderful man.
I think our town thought a lot about my uncle-- a lot.
He was a nice man.
Everybody was nice.
I mean, there were-- they were just, I think a lot of people looked after them in our town.
That's my opinion.
That's my opinion.
They were always doing things for other people.
They were always supporting, giving for the poor.
That was-- that was my family.
Yes.
I remember.
Once the war broke out, that's all we had, was always refugees.
Always people coming and coming and coming.
The people that the Germans didn't bring, they came by themselves.
Meserich was, I don't know, it was situated on a closed railroad line.
And I guess maybe that what brought them.
And then Meserich was a-- a Jewish town, and I guess the people just came there.
People from all-- from all around Meserich, from the little towns from the country things, they kept coming to the city, because everybody wanted to be closer to a town.
So they left their places, and they'd come to the city.
But most of the people that came to Meserich came because the Germans just brought them there.
And they'd bring people from all the way up from Warsaw, from one side of Warsaw.
Then they'd bring people from Czechoslovakia.
Then they'd bring people from Hungary.
Then they'd bring people from [NON-ENGLISH].
Then they'd bring people from [NON-ENGLISH].
Then they'd-- they brought people from, I think, in our-- a lot of people from different towns that I can't remember.
A lot, a lot of different towns.
Smaller and larger.
The people were coming from Warsaw, going through.
People were just kept coming.
About 142 kilometers.
Yeah.
We used to hear it from the news-- the newspapers, the radios.
Everybody knew what was going on.
As young as I was, we knew that the Germans were taking over this and taking over all the different places, and they displaced people, and they were killing people.
We heard about the Crystal Night.
We heard-- we heard about everything.
There was nothing hidden.
Everybody knew what going on.
But nobody had enough sense to leave Poland or Europe.
I guess everybody thought it's going to blow over, it's going to blow over.
Maybe there's going to be no war.
People kept talking there's going to be a war, and there's not going to be a war.
But nobody knew the hell that we were going to have to go through.
Because nobody expected that a person like Hitler would do this to 6 million Jews.
And so many millions of other people.
We just didn't realize what a person could do to-- to another person.
Well, when the war broke out, the Germans came in, by September 1, 1939.
And they came in, I think-- I think, September 2nd to our town.
And they were in our town about a week to 10 days.
Then they left, and the Russians came in, because before the-- the first war, Meserich used to be, I think it belonged either to Russia or to Austria.
I'm not sure.
To one of those.
And then the-- the Russians came in because they had a pact with Hitler.
And they came into Meserich.
And they were in Meserich for about 10-- 10 to 11 days over Sukkots.
A little of a Sukkots.
And then the Germans-- the Russians left that time again, because they decided that the border is going to be by the Bug.
That's the-- that's a river that goes through 64 kilometers away from Meserich.
And that's where they made the border, the river.
And the Russians left.
And when the Russians left, about 1,500 Jewish families left with them.
And the rest is history.
We stayed in Meserich and we took everything that the Germans dished out to us.
Well, like I said before, I took in a family.
And then we were-- my aunts were constantly cooking.
And we and my cousins, we used to take parts of food and-- you know, rolls and stuff.
We used to take to the synagogues or to [NON-ENGLISH] where they used to keep them when they first came in, because they were always hungry.
They were always without food for so many days before they brought them in.
And then not my family, but they all did.
The Judenrat was trying to find places for them to live.
And there was-- in the beginning, it was easier to find places for them where to live around with people.
But later on, it got so crowded, there was really no place where to put them.
Until just before the first action started, they were kept bringing and bringing people from all over.
The Polish-- what do you call it-- magistrate says there must have been through Meserich about 38,000 people went through.
Yeah.
So it's not just a few people.
And they kept coming and going and coming and going.
And it was always-- I mean, everybody was sleeping three, four, five people in a room.
There was no place where to put them.
And-- but I think Meserich did the best they could for the refugees.
To where?
To Russia?
Sure.
I heard about a lot of families that people were going.
People were running across the Bug, running away from the Germans, going.
People-- people were always on the go.
On the go and coming.
This one didn't want to leave the family, so he came back.
This one left the family, then decided that's not good-- no good to be on his own.
So they came back again.
People were always on the go.
Nobody stayed put.
I don't think anybody.
It was very few.
The old one and the children.
The people that had small children and they couldn't go, that-- they had to be, you know, stay home.
Well, everybody was on edge.
Everybody was on edge.
Because people didn't know what to do with themself.
And they didn't know what to do.
They went to do one thing, and it turned out good, so they were happy for a little bit.
And then it turned out different.
And when the Germans came in, when they started, always-- always they took out 25, 50 people, and killed them before they told you what they wanted.
They didn't just say, give me-- they wanted from Meserich a lot of gold.
But before that, they killed a lot of Jews.
Then, when it came out when they started the war with the Russians, they said they decided that we have to give them away all the furs.
Everything that has to do with fur, we have to give them away.
So before they did that, they came in and they killed a lot of people.
And one of the people killed was my uncle.
They walked just plain into the house, and they took him into the other room, and they killed him.
And my aunt and her children had to watch it.
Afterwards for a while, they-- they said what they wanted.
And Meserich always gave them what they wanted.
But they never-- they were so bloodthirsty that they always killed before they told you what they wanted.
I-- I don't think there's enough written about Meserich.
Because there were so many people going through Meserich, coming there, seeking refuge, and getting-- getting refuge, and getting things, as much as people could.
They always tried to help those people.
But the people didn't-- I don't know.
Maybe there's books and things written about Meserich that I don't know.
I know there's one book written about Meserich, but it's written in Hebrew and in Yiddish.
And we were very much against it.
We wanted it to be written in English, too, just because our children, you know, cannot speak Yiddish or something.
The English comes easier to them.
But they were trying to translate it to English, but it never came about.
One thing that Meserich is doing now is they're trying to build a fence around the cemetery.
There was one cemetery that the Germans just took apart, that was the old cemetery.
Then there was the newer cemetery, and they took everything out, all the [INAUDIBLE] and the, you know, headstones, and the things.
They took everything apart.
And they made like sidewalks out of it.
One of the ladies from Meserich which resided in the United States, her-- her name was Sarah Finkelstein.
Her husband, they gave a large donation to Meserich to begin with to pick up all those stone-- headstones and things.
And they made something out of this on the cemetery.
Now they're trying to build a fence around the cemetery.
And I think the Polish government from Meserich is working with them on it.
They hope to have it done.
It's in progress.
So my cousin is very active with it.
And she wants to go to see if they did it.
So maybe in the future, I'll go.
I don't feel like going back there.
I don't-- I just don't have the heart to go back there.
Right.
But if she wants to go, I think I'll go with her.
I wouldn't let her go by herself.
You bring it back to the Zionist, what he was.
He was just a young boy.
What can he do?
He was, what do you call it, 12, 13 years old.
He didn't do nothing.
He just had an idea that that's what he wanted, and that's what he did.
And once when he grew up and when he went to Israel, that was his-- his wish was always just to immigrate to Israel, as young as he was.
You couldn't make him come here.
There was always resist, people that resist.
There were always people that getting together and making plans where to go away and where to hide.

[INAUDIBLE] there was by the time we find out actually in 1942, when the Russian-- the Russian war broke out, the German-Russian, that's when we started to believe that they're going to take us to different [? stands ?] back into Russia, just like they were bringing the other Jews to us.
Not that they occupied the other places.
They were going to take us there.
Because nobody thought of anything else until two weeks before Rosh Hashanah, 1942, when it was the first action.
And that's when the people that ran away, they took them to Treblinka.
And I know of three people that came back from there.
And they-- when they came back, they told-- told us of all the horrors that were-- that were happening.
They still didn't know at the time about the gas chambers and things.
But they were telling, they picked up so many people and they pushed them in the [INAUDIBLE], you know, the railroads, and the things, and they packed them in like herrings.
The people couldn't breathe.
There's a lot of people died right then and there.
But this lady and her five-year-old son, she just walked away from Treblinka and came back, died last year in the United States.
And her son is still alive.
And there was another man that came back to us much later-- that was in 1943-- that ran away from Treblinka.
When the Germans were here?
Or you mean before the war?
Before the war, my-- my uncle that used to have-- help me a lot with my brother, that he wanted, in the worstest way he wanted to leave Poland.
In the worsted way.
But it just didn't work out.
So what.
I had an aunt that left Poland with her husband and her youngest son.
They went to Australia.
So if it's not meant to live.
She arrived there.
She died there right away.
She had a heart attack because she left two more children in Poland, and she went.
So it didn't help.
If it didn't meant to be alive, you just went wherever you went.
Was there any opportunity to flee into Russia, or was there danger there also?
A lot of people went to Russia.
Not legally.
Everybody went, you know, through the river Bug.
They had places where there was-- the river was narrow, and they could, you know, escape.
There was a lot of people that knew the roads out there, how to take them back.
They had to smuggle us, they say.
There was a lot of people gone.
Yeah.
Life just went on as before.
After they-- after the war-- there was no war.
Nothing happened.
The people said, OK.
Now he's got Czechoslovakia, and that's all.
He's not going to bother us anymore.
They thought that he's going to have enough.
But how wrong can they get?
But nobody-- nobody was worried.
I guess anybody that wanted to go at that time Palestine, people couldn't get passports to go.
Anyplace you wanted to go, there was-- you had to have a lot of money to leave Poland.
And you had to have somebody to send you a visa.
You couldn't just take up and go.
And there was very few people left.
Very few people.
Music, yeah.
Jewish theater.
Everything.
Everything was, I think.
Everything.
Meserich was a very nice Jewish town.
[WHISPERING] We're still going to talk about Meserich.
I hope our children remember.
I try to tell my kids everything that I know.
And my children are very interested in Meserich.
And we talk quite often about it.
We always reminisce about the times past.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
I remember.
Every summer, we used to go to the country.
We never stayed in Meserich.
We always used to go to the country every single summer.
Then on bicycles, we'd go back to school, because school wasn't out yet.
We'd ride the bicycles back to school for the month, or three weeks, whatever time there was left, depending on the weather.
And we used to go there.
We used to stay there all summer.
It wasn't far away from Meserich.
It was just about five kilometers.
We used to love it there, because it was-- we used to run around and do things.
Go picking berries.
I really, I don't know what to say about it.
I loved being in Meserich.
I loved the people that I knew.
I don't know.
[SIGHS] [WHISPERING]
